segments.text:
HEPATITIS B
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

Hepatitis B
Double-stranded DNA virus
E nveloped
Hepadnoviridae family – attach to hepatocytes (liver cells) in humans, mammals, and birds
Four serotypes – adr , adw , ayr , ayw
Eight genotypes – A-H
Based on proteins in outer envelope
Genotypes differ by more than 8%
Used to monitor spread

Transmission
Transmitted through activities that involve percutaneous (skin puncture) or mucosal contact with infectious blood or body fluids (semen and saliva)
Sex with infected partner
Injection drug use – sharing needles, syringes, or drug-preparation equipment
Contact with blood or open sores of infected person
Exposure to needle sticks or sharp instruments
Sharing items that can break skin or mucous membranes – razors, toothbrushes, glucose monitoring equipment
Birth from infected mother
H 0 = 5 when no intervention is seen

Anyone can get hepatitis B, but some are at greater risk
Infants born to infected mothers
Injection drug users – sharing needles, syringes, and other drug equipment
Sex partners of people with hepatitis B
Men who have sex with men
People living with someone who has hepatitis B
Healthcare and public-safety workers exposed to blood on job
Hemodialysis patients
At Greater Risk

Acute Hepatitis B
Short term illness – within first 6 months of exposure
Most children <5yr and newly infected immunocompetent adults have no symptoms
30% - 50% of people ≥5yr have symptoms
Fever
Fatigue
Loss of appetite
Nausea
Vomiting
90% clear infection without treatment
Abdominal pain
Dark urine
Clay-colored stool
Joint pain
Jaundice

Chronic Hepatitis B
Acute hepatitis B  chronic hepatitis B
Over time – liver damage, cirrhosis, liver cancer, death
25% who become chronically ill in childhood and 15% who become chronically ill after childhood
Die prematurely from cirrhosis or liver cancer
Most remain asymptomatic until cirrhosis or end-stage liver disease show up

Diagnosing Hepatitis B
HBsAg – surface antigen
Most reliable marker
Found weeks before evidence of liver damage
IgM anti-HBc – core antigen
Appears early in the course of disease
Anti-HBs – 6 months after acute infection
Indicates chronic carrier
Best indication of active viral replication – HBV DNA
Quantitation predicts patient response to treatment

Hepatitis B Vaccine Timeline
First vaccine (plama-derived) licensed in US
Second recombinant vaccine licensed
Plasma-derived vaccine removed from market
Pediarix vaccine licensed
1981
1989
1992
2002
1986
First recombinant vaccine licensed in US
2001
Twinrix vaccine licensed

Recombinant Vaccines
Plasmid (chunk of DNA) containing gene for HBsAg into baker’s yeast ( Saccharomyces cerevisiae )
Vaccine contains >95% HBsAg and up to 5% yeast protein
Yeast makes HBsAg – harvested and  purified
Dose | Usual Age
1 | Birth
2 | 1-2 months
3 | 6-18 months
Infant Schedule
Dose | Usual Interval
1 | --
2 | 1 month
3 | 5 months
Adult Schedule

Single Antigen Vaccine Options
Recombivax HB
(Merck)
Pediatric and adult
Single-dose vial and syringe
Aluminum hydrophosphate sulfate adjuvant
Engerix -B
(GlaxoSmithKline)
Pediatric and adult
Single-dose vial and syringe
Aluminum hydroxide adjuvant
Efficacy – 95% (range: 80% - 100%)
Immunity – 20 years or more

Combination Vaccine Options
Pediarix
(GlaxoSmithKline)
Combination of DTaP-Hep B-IPV
3 doses – 2, 4, 6 mo
Approved for children 6 weeks to 7 years
May be used when mothers are HBsAg positive or unknown
Twinrix
(GlaxoSmithKline)
Combination of Hep A and Hep B
3 doses – 0, 1, 6 mo
Also approved for 18+ when Hep A and Hep B are needed
